exercises.title: Reading the real thing
exercises.body: Time to read like a Shavian reader: a whole passage on one side, and you writing out the English on the other. The text is “The North Wind and the Sun” — the Aesop fable that linguists have used for over a century to show off writing systems. Small slips of case or punctuation are fine; just write what it says.

exercises.passage: 𐑞 𐑯𐑹𐑔 𐑢𐑦𐑯𐑛 𐑯 𐑞 𐑕𐑳𐑯 𐑢𐑻 𐑛𐑦𐑕𐑐𐑿𐑑𐑦𐑙 𐑢𐑦𐑗 𐑢𐑪𐑟 𐑞 𐑕𐑑𐑮𐑪𐑙𐑜𐑼.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: the north wind and the sun were disputing which was the stronger
exercises.correctLabel: the north wind and the sun were disputing which was the stronger
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.passage: 𐑢𐑧𐑯 𐑩 𐑑𐑮𐑨𐑝𐑩𐑤𐑼 𐑒𐑱𐑥 𐑩𐑤𐑪𐑙 𐑮𐑨𐑐𐑑 𐑦𐑯 𐑩 𐑢𐑹𐑥 𐑒𐑤𐑴𐑒.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: when a traveller came along wrapped in a warm cloak
exercises.correctLabel: when a traveller came along wrapped in a warm cloak
accept: when a traveler came along wrapped in a warm cloak
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.title: The wager
exercises.body: They agree on a test: {{𐑞 𐑢𐑳𐑯 𐑣𐑵 𐑓𐑻𐑕𐑑 𐑕𐑩𐑒𐑕𐑰𐑛𐑩𐑛 𐑦𐑯 𐑥𐑱𐑒𐑦𐑙 𐑞 𐑑𐑮𐑨𐑝𐑩𐑤𐑼 𐑑𐑱𐑒 𐑣𐑦𐑟 𐑒𐑤𐑴𐑒 𐑪𐑓 𐑖𐑫𐑛 𐑚𐑰 𐑒𐑩𐑯𐑕𐑦𐑛𐑼𐑛 𐑕𐑑𐑮𐑪𐑙𐑜𐑼 𐑞𐑨𐑯 𐑞 𐑳𐑞𐑼.}} Whoever first makes the traveller take his cloak off should be considered stronger than the other.

exercises.passage: 𐑞𐑧𐑯 𐑞 𐑯𐑹𐑔 𐑢𐑦𐑯𐑛 𐑚𐑤𐑵 𐑨𐑟 𐑣𐑸𐑛 𐑨𐑟 𐑣𐑰 𐑒𐑫𐑛.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: then the north wind blew as hard as he could
exercises.correctLabel: then the north wind blew as hard as he could
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.passage: 𐑚𐑳𐑑 𐑞 𐑥𐑹 𐑣𐑰 𐑚𐑤𐑵, 𐑞 𐑥𐑹 𐑒𐑤𐑴𐑕𐑤𐑦 𐑛𐑦𐑛 𐑞 𐑑𐑮𐑨𐑝𐑩𐑤𐑼 𐑓𐑴𐑤𐑛 𐑣𐑦𐑟 𐑒𐑤𐑴𐑒 𐑼𐑬𐑯𐑛 𐑣𐑦𐑥.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: but the more he blew the more closely did the traveller fold his cloak around him
exercises.correctLabel: but the more he blew the more closely did the traveller fold his cloak around him
accept: but the more he blew the more closely did the traveler fold his cloak around him
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.passage: 𐑯 𐑨𐑑 𐑤𐑭𐑕𐑑 𐑞 𐑯𐑹𐑔 𐑢𐑦𐑯𐑛 𐑜𐑱𐑝 𐑳𐑐 𐑞 𐑩𐑑𐑧𐑥𐑐𐑑.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: and at last the north wind gave up the attempt
exercises.correctLabel: and at last the north wind gave up the attempt
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.title: Now the sun
exercises.body: The wind has failed. Watch how little the sun has to do — and keep writing.

exercises.passage: 𐑞𐑧𐑯 𐑞 𐑕𐑳𐑯 𐑖𐑪𐑯 𐑬𐑑 𐑢𐑹𐑥𐑤𐑦, 𐑯 𐑦𐑥𐑰𐑛𐑾𐑑𐑤𐑦 𐑞 𐑑𐑮𐑨𐑝𐑩𐑤𐑼 𐑑𐑫𐑒 𐑪𐑓 𐑣𐑦𐑟 𐑒𐑤𐑴𐑒.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: then the sun shone out warmly and immediately the traveller took off his cloak
exercises.correctLabel: then the sun shone out warmly and immediately the traveller took off his cloak
accept: then the sun shone out warmly and immediately the traveler took off his cloak
then the sun shined out warmly and immediately the traveller took off his cloak
then the sun shined out warmly and immediately the traveler took off his cloak
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.passage: 𐑯 𐑕𐑴 𐑞 𐑯𐑹𐑔 𐑢𐑦𐑯𐑛 𐑢𐑪𐑟 𐑩𐑚𐑤𐑲𐑡𐑛 𐑑 𐑒𐑩𐑯𐑓𐑧𐑕 𐑞𐑨𐑑 𐑞 𐑕𐑳𐑯 𐑢𐑪𐑟 𐑞 𐑕𐑑𐑮𐑪𐑙𐑜𐑼 𐑝 𐑞 𐑑𐑵.
exercises.caption: Read the passage, then write it out in English
exercises.correct: and so the north wind was obliged to confess that the sun was the stronger of the two
exercises.correctLabel: and so the north wind was obliged to confess that the sun was the stronger of the two
exercises.source: Aesop, “The North Wind and the Sun”

exercises.prompt: In the fable, who turned out to be the stronger?

exercises.title: You read a real text
exercises.body: That was a genuine piece of literature, read straight from Shavian and written out in your own hand — the full journey of Chapter 3. Chapter 4 will turn it around and teach you to write Shavian yourself. Until then, the Resources page has whole books ready for you.
